summary: Gruber analyzes the preliminary WWDC 2012 schedule for clues about Apple's plans, treating it as a Kremlinology exercise. He notes two significant changes: the Apple Design Awards being moved to a prime Monday afternoon slot (suggesting Apple wants to emphasize third-party app quality as a competitive advantage over Android), and an unusually high number of 'To Be Announced' sessions filling the largest room for days. He speculates this pattern is consistent with Apple announcing a new developer platform, possibly apps for Apple TV, and connects this to Tim Cook's recent comments about Apple TV sales growth and BGR's report about a new TV OS demo.
key_points: The Apple Design Awards moved from Wednesday evening to Monday afternoon, one of only three events all 5,000 attendees will see, signaling Apple's intent to promote third-party app quality as a competitive weapon against Android
An unusually high number of TBA sessions fill Presidio (the largest room) for two and a half days, suggesting something bigger than typical incremental announcements
The pattern of TBA sessions resembles what you'd expect if Apple were launching an entirely new developer platform, like apps for Apple TV
Apple TV sales were on pace for 100% year-over-year growth, making it ripe for an App Store expansion
Gruber frames all Apple analysis as Kremlinology — reading between the lines of an opaque organization's few public signals
money_quotes: I've long compared Apple punditry to Cold War-era Kremlinology — to predict or analyze an opaque, secretive organization, you've got to read between the lines of the few things they do say, and you've got to know how to interpret silence.
So, let's go deep and severely overanalyze and jump to unfounded conclusions based on two things that struck me regarding the just-released-yesterday preliminary schedule for WWDC 2012.
There are only three events on the schedule for Monday, all three of which will have the attention of all 5,000 attendees, and Apple is using one of them to promote third-party apps.
This implies not just that Apple will be announcing new stuff (duh, it's WWDC), but new stuff that will fill the biggest room in the building with two-and-a-half days worth of sessions.

Of course, this is Apple, so this could be nothing. Could well be that when the TBAs are filled in on the conference schedule on Monday June 11, we'll look at these sessions and have no idea why they were 'TBA' on the preliminary schedule. Secrecy just for the sake of secrecy.
Apple's 'hobby' is catching on, and I can't think of anything Apple could do to make it more interesting and popular than to open a new branch of the App Store for it.
key_insight: The unusually high number of TBA sessions filling WWDC's largest room for days suggests Apple is preparing to announce something as significant as an entirely new developer platform, not just iterative updates.
